title: Reasoning on a Spectrum: Aligning LLMs to System 1 and System 2 Thinking

reviews.summary: The paper proposes aligning large language models (LLMs) to two distinct reasoning modes inspired by dual-process theory: fast, heuristic System 1 and slow, analytical System 2. The authors curate a 2,000-example dataset where each prompt has paired, valid System 1 and System 2 responses grounded in 10 cognitive heuristics, and they train preference-aligned models using DPO and SimPO. Across 13 benchmarks, they find an accuracy-efficiency trade-off: System 2 alignment improves arithmetic and symbolic reasoning, while System 1 alignment performs better on commonsense tasks; interpolating between styles yields monotonic performance transitions, and uncertainty analyses suggest System 2 exhibits greater hedging and lower token-level confidence.
strengths: Positions LLM reasoning explicitly within dual-process theory and operationalizes the distinction via preference alignment to System 1/2 responses.

Introduces a curated dataset pairing “valid” System 1 and System 2 outputs across 10 cognitive heuristics, with length-matched pairs to reduce confounding from response length during preference optimization.
Presents a simple but insightful interpolation strategy between System 1 and System 2 preferences to study the continuity of reasoning behaviors and accuracy along a spectrum.

Evaluates on a diverse battery of 13 benchmarks spanning arithmetic, symbolic, and commonsense reasoning, with consistent patterns across base models (Llama-3-8B-Instruct and Mistral-7B-Instruct) and two preference algorithms (DPO, SimPO).
Includes analyses beyond raw accuracy: response length, uncertainty via logits and hedging, and “definitiveness” via an LLM-as-judge.
Reports statistical tests for several behavioral comparisons (length, hedging, uncertainty), and attempts to control a common artifact (length bias) during dataset curation.
The framing is clear, and the main claims are consistently tied to the dual-process perspective.
The methodological pipeline for dataset creation (generation, refinement, verification) and the overall alignment setup are described at a level that is easy to follow.
Provides a compact and general lens—fast vs. slow reasoning—under which common empirical observations (e.g., overthinking, verbosity) become interpretable and actionable.
The results challenge the assumption that step-by-step reasoning is always beneficial and motivate task-adaptive reasoning strategies.
weaknesses: The 2K-pair dataset, while carefully curated, is small; reliance on GPT-4o generation plus 20% expert revisions raises concerns about style leakage and validity of “both responses are correct” across items.

Preference alignment may still latch onto stylistic markers (hedges, modality, tone) rather than substantive reasoning differences; the uncertainty and hedge analyses could partially reflect learned style rather than cognitive process.

The uncertainty analysis uses “sum log probabilities” in Figure 4A (per caption), which is length-sensitive; even if lengths are closer in stage 1, stage-2 differences persist and confound comparisons. Average per-token log-probability or entropy would be more appropriate.
No variance across training seeds or multiple fine-tuning runs; many accuracy gains are modest and could fall within run-to-run variance.
Limited baselines: only zero-shot and zero-shot CoT are reported; stronger inference-time baselines (self-consistency, ToT, s1-style scaling) would contextualize benefits more rigorously.
The “efficiency” claim is not quantified in terms of wall-clock latency or FLOPs per output—only token lengths; compute-vs-accuracy trade-off curves would strengthen the central claim.

The interpolation study is promising but reported on a subset in the main text; full results are deferred to the appendix, and it is unclear whether monotonicity holds universally or with statistical significance across all tasks.

Some examples of System 1 “validity” read more as opinionated or overconfident text than verifiably correct answers; clearer task definitions and correctness criteria for both responses would build confidence.

Several implementation details are missing or scattered (e.g., fine-tuning hyperparameters, LoRA or full fine-tune, selection of interpolation ratios, early stopping criteria, decoding parameters across benchmarks).

The paper cites dual-process-inspired frameworks and prompting strategies but lacks empirical comparisons to dynamic routing or hybrid controllers that choose when to “think more” (e.g., role-play prompting variants, self-evaluation routing, or test-time scaling methods).
Connections to calibration metrics (ECE/Brier) and selective prediction (abstention, deferral) are relevant given the uncertainty findings but not explored.
reviews.questions:
1. How do you ensure that both System 1 and System 2 responses are truly “valid” for each example? Did you perform human correctness annotations beyond expert conformance to heuristic definitions? Please report inter-annotator agreement and an error analysis of mislabeled or non-valid pairs.
2. For the uncertainty analysis, did you compute average per-token log-probabilities or entropy to mitigate length confounds, especially in stage 2? If not, what do the results look like with length-normalized uncertainty metrics?
3. Can you report results across multiple fine-tuning seeds and include confidence intervals for benchmark accuracies? Which gains remain statistically robust?
4. What are the fine-tuning details (LoRA vs. full fine-tune, hyperparameters, number of steps, batch sizes) and decoding settings (temperature, top-p, max tokens) used across experiments? How sensitive are the results to decoding parameters?
5. Could you compare against stronger inference-time baselines (e.g., self-consistency, ToT, or test-time scaling) and provide compute-normalized comparisons (accuracy vs. tokens/latency)?
6. In the interpolation study, do you observe any tasks where performance is non-monotonic, and how do you explain deviations if any? Please include error bars and statistical tests in the main paper for representative tasks.
7. Beyond hedging frequency, did you evaluate calibration quality (e.g., ECE/Brier) and hallucination rates for System 1 vs. System 2? This would better connect uncertainty behavior to reliability.
8. How well do results generalize to larger models (e.g., 13B/70B) and other preference optimizers or RLHF variants? Any preliminary evidence?
reviews.assessment:
This paper presents a compelling and well-argued case that reasoning style matters: aligning LLMs to System 1 versus System 2 produces predictable trade-offs across task families, with System 2 excelling at arithmetic/symbolic reasoning and System 1 doing better on commonsense. The dataset and the interpolation experiments are thoughtful contributions that move the discussion beyond “more CoT is always better.” The work is timely and practically relevant.

That said, the empirical evidence—though suggestive—has notable gaps. The small dataset, potential residual stylistic confounds, length-sensitive uncertainty metrics, limited baselines, and lack of multiple-seed reporting reduce confidence in the strength and generality of the claims. The methodology is incremental (preference alignment with standard methods), and while the framing is novel, stronger experimental controls and compute-normalized comparisons are needed to meet the bar of a top-tier venue.

Overall, I view this as a promising direction and a useful analysis contribution. With additional rigor—especially around uncertainty measurement, stronger baselines, calibration/robustness evaluation, and multi-seed reporting—it could make a solid NeurIPS paper. In its current form, I lean toward borderline accept, appreciating the conceptual clarity and diagnostic insights, but encourage substantial strengthening of the empirical validation.
